Observation Details: Along Asilomar Avenue, I followed a begging crow giving begging cries almost constantly. At first the begging bird was on a phone wire, and another crow appeared and seemed to copulate with it, and then flew off. The begging crow continued to give begging cries for a long period, first perched in an oak and then on the ground, as which point a pair of (presumed) adults joined it on the ground, but did not appear to feed it (except, maybe, something small left on the ground?). I audio-taped this interaction, in which the begging crow was continually calling but gave a gurgle when one crow dropped something (and the begger ate it?). The begging crow looked a bit smaller and decidedly browner than the two, presumably-paired, adults. Today's date is a full month prior to the earliest date of adults feeding young in Monterey County (i.e., 4 May) and even earlier than full fledglings. This crow could fly -- and easily did so to cross the street and to come back. After research in our Breeding Bird Atlas, and in Cornell's on-line BoW, I'm inclined to think the call was this one, as described by BoW: "Food-Begging Call. A drawn-out, loud, monotonous caa, given at intervals by female only, on the nest or away from it, prior to egg-laying, and during incubation, and audible at ≥300 m; closely resembles begging call of older nestlings and fledglings."  This may serve as a solicitation call by a female prior to egg-laying (details for the "Northwestern Crow"" set out this more explicitly). I think my initial reaction -- solicitation and copulation --  was correct, and not may later thought as a first-of-season begging fledgling. I did not see a gular flange as a fledgling would have had.

Observation Details: Census of nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, OR. This is the greatest number of this roosting season. The vast majority of the crows are in the south/east portion of the survey area with a small number near the central portion of the area. The crows were largely settled and little movement was seen during the 3+ hours of surveying. I checked the Pearl District, the area north of the downtown core where 1,000+ crows were regularly found roosting in the past, but there were no crows and no signs (i.e., droppings) of recent significant activity. I also checked frequent roosting areas in the north section of the survey area, and at PSU, but no crows were observed. Crows were roosting on the roof of the Keller Auditorium and the top deck of the Crown Plaza parking garage.

Media notes: Crows apparently trying to chase off another crow.  A gull cries in the background.
Observation Details: There were some crows calling loudly in the trees near me.  I moved closer to try to see the source of their concern. Two more crows flew in and also called, while others around me joined in the calling.  I think there were 6 or 7 crows in this area.  Two crows flew out of the tree, and one of them attacked the other by dropping onto it from above to hit it with its feet. Chasing continued off and on and appeared to involve 3 crows trying to chase off 1 crow, while others called from the trees.  The chase moved from one tree to another, but the apparently offending crow was never driven off in the 10 minutes I watched.

Media notes: A dozen or more crows mobbing a great horned owl. The volume varies according to the relative positions of the birds and recordist, who start about 250-300' apart. Partway through the recording the owl, pursued by the crows, flies almost directly over the recordist,  who subsequently walked to a position quite close to the owls and crows. (Neigbors come out to see and discuss the commotion.) The owl then flies away again, pursued by the crows.
Observation Details: About a dozen crows making a huge racket on the top of a tree on Menlo Boulevard. Eventually a great horned owl flew out into a spruce tree in Newton Avenue followed by the crows. Lateral barring of the underside of the owl was clearly visible. After another several minutes the owl flew south pursued by the crows and the mobbing sound continued from what seemed to be about three blocks away. After ten minutes or so the calling was  no longer audible, but I had not noticed when it had ceased.

Media notes: A crow called ahead of me. As I approached, it flew into a tree ahead of me. It did this a few times, then flew to a tree off the trail (1:40) and lit near a Great Horned Owl.  It seemed to be leading me to the owl.
Observation Details: I heard a crow making what sounded like alarm calls.  As I got closer to it, it would fly into a tree ahead of me on the trail.  It did this 3 or 4 times, then flew to a tree off the trail.  When I looked in that direction, I saw the Great Horned Owl.  Then the crow flew very close to the owl and landed on the same branch it was on.  After a few minutes, the crow flew off.  No other crows ever came to join it.  It seemed as thought the crow was trying to draw my attention to it and be sure the owl was seen.

Media notes: A flock of 20+ American Crows were apparently mobbing something in a cypress tree. What initially caught my attention was the squalling of one particular crow, the same vocalization that Frank Goodwin and I heard and recorded at the beginning of the trail (near Camps Canal) on 11 September 2021: https://ebird.org/checklist/S94508097  Shortly thereafter the calling of the mob grew louder and more frantic, and I watched carefully to see if an owl or hawk would fly from the tree. Instead, a crow flew out, closely pursued by 20 other crows (an indeterminate number remained in the cypress tree). Although I was watching carefully, I saw nothing precede the first crow. The entire recording was nearly four minutes, so I clipped out a couple of samples and edited them together, separated only with a one-second span of silence.

Observation Details: 5 crows (3 that were primarily hanging out on building across street, 1 that was hanging out on building caddy corner, and 1 hanging out on building across the street but down on corner overhang) and all were cawing obnoxiously and seemingly in some sort of high tension mode. One specifically kept flying to just above my building and flying away, while cawing very loudly, and at first didn't know what had them so riled up but then a little later, there was another black bird that came swooping from my building roof that did not appear to be on friendly terms with the crows making all the racket at it, and it then just flew up and dove down behind building across street. I think it either was an enemy crow, or possibly a raven. And then soon after that, the crows still didn't seem to be calming down and still doing their aggressive charges from building across street to the roof of my building, and then another big black bird came swooping out from my building roof and flew past really fast headed in same direction as the one previously did. Did not get good look at these two enemy corvids and my photos are not best quality to ascertain whether also crows or ravens, so will put them down in the crow/raven category. The rest of the crows still seemed in high tension mode for a while, but their cawing became less intense/loud and the one that was really going at it, seemed to stop doing the charge attack after the second big black bird flew over and left the area. See photos and audio.
Estimate.  Most flew in and stopped for a short time in the trees, then flew on.

Media notes: This is a monaural recording of the calls of several hundred American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) as they were staging in the darkness of predawn, next to their winter roost.  The animals were approximately 75 meters from the microphone.  The habitat was mature, leafless trees at the edge of a blacktop parking lot at a factory in downtown Meadville, PA. The temperature was 33 degrees and it was spitting wet snow. It was recorded at 0635 hrs.,  on 17 January 2021.  This recording was captured by James R. Hill, III, using a Telinga parabola with one Sennheiser MKH-20 microphone and a Sound Designs MixPre-3 digital recorder.
Observation Details: There were thousands of crows leaving their roost trees and flying overhead, then perching/staging in the bare trees at the north end of the Channellock Factory parking lot.  Many photos and videos were captured of this phenomenon, plus a sound recording made.   We estimated there were at least 2000 crows.  These photos show only a fraction of the total number of crows in this roost.  This roost has been forming in this area for many winters now.  Several of the bare trees they stage in, have plastic crow decoys hanging upside-down in them, depicting dead, hanging crows.  These hanging decoys are meant to scare the crows out of the trees.  Obviously, the decoys don't work!  Decoy photos taken 24 November 2020, hanging in the same tree as in the previous photos.

Observation Details: Audio recordings are of two separate American Crows. Both were perched together in a tree not more than 50 feet from me.  I recorded for about 5 minutes. For most of the time one crow called and then the second crow with the higher voice called for about a minute or two before they both flew off.   I separated the recording into two files in order to separate the recordings of the two birds.  They seemed to be calling to other more distant crows.  The more distant crows called back but I'm not sure they can be heard in the recording.  One crow had a higher voice than the other.  Recordings were made on an iPhone with the RODE Rec App and edited (Normalized and High-Pass Filter) using Audacity.

Observation Details: I first heard a loud cawing ruckus from what seemed to be a gang of angry American Crows. I then saw a bunch of them on a tree, and they appeared to be mobbing something. At one point, while they were flying, I thought I saw a bigger bird that seemed to be what they were chasing. Finally, the Great Horned Owl came in clear view as the American Crows continued to harass it.

Observation Details:
After seeing three ravens well separated foraging in nearby pasture, I soon walked around the corner at Downie and what I thought was another flew up from foraging along the road into deciduous saplings. It probably called to the others, “here’s that two-legger again with the odd hat that keeps pointing some thin object at us” and then started laughing. Later, after posting this as a raven, another birder I contacted suggested it was crow rather than raven. Listening again and comparing to Cornell files had me agreeing with him.
 Habitat: pasture land; mixed forest with young deciduous saplings and older evergreens, as well as some tall cottonwoods; somewhat riparian.

Observation Details: At first, two or three crows were calling from the southwest corner of the woods, moving from place to place in the upper canopy of one or two trees, They subsequently moved north, still on the edge of the woods, making a great deal of noise. It became apparent that they were mobbing a red-tailed hawk that eventually called and moved further north, to the northwest corner of the central woods. The crows calmed down somewhat, but picked up the harassment at a lower level, then upped the harrassment, causing the hawk to move once again. First three recordings are uploaded in order. The third includes the intense mobbing. I initiated a recording of the intense mobbing with Merlin on an iPhone before starting a recording with a Zoom F3 and a Rode NTG2. The iPhone recording (final uploaded file) thus overlaps the time period of the ZoomF3 mobbing recording, starting about 45 seconds sooner. (I inadvertently also uploaded the untrimmed and unamplified Merlin / iPhone recording (fourth upload) and thus far have left it for audio quality comparison.)

I debated posting this because i know that Fish Crow has never been recorded in NB. I heard (and then saw) a strange sounding crow near the Smythe St. entrance to Odell Park. The call was high pitched, nasally, and more abrupt than a typical AMCR. The audio recording attached is poor quality, taken from my cell phone. I heard the bird again the next morning in the same location. It seems to be hanging around if anyone wanted to come down and try to confirm this report. Im happy to be proven wrong if this is just an odd sounding AMCR. The first recording is from the day of this observation. The second recording is a bit better, taken the next day (15 Apri 2023) in the same location. There is less noise on the second recording and the call is more clear. Update. Thanks to Jim Wilson for identifying this as a female AMCR nesting call.

As I was waking up in the morning, still half-asleep, I started hearing the distinct calling of a crow. It’s “caw-caw-caw” fooled me into thinking I was somewhere on the coast. Sadly, I rolled over in bed, opened my eyes, and still saw the mound of snow covering my windows. “I was dreaming. I didn’t hear a crow,” I thought to myself. Yet, the “caw-caw-caw”‘s kept piercing through my window. I got up, walked outside and found a lone crow beside two turkey vultures! I wasn’t dreaming (at least about the bird)! Medium sized corvid with all black plumage. The bird’s bill was noticeable tighter and smaller than a raven’s and the bird was giving it’s diagnostic “caw-caw-caw” call. Will upload photos and audio.

Estimate. I heard Crows at a distance when I started my walk. Sounded like they were agitated. They went quiet. Later, the Crows came down the hill in a big mob, calling like crazy. I thought there must be a Great Horned Owl. Crazy Mob! I took a spot and waited, hoping the target would come my way. There was a move and the mob went nuts. There was another move and the mob went bonkers again. I was scanning the tall evergreens but could not find anything. On the third move I caught  glimpse. Pretty sure it was Great Horned Owl. Just a large, dark bird flying low and away from Crows. I lost some time waiting on this. Eventually the whole mass moved down to the tall pines near Beech St.. I tried again to spot but no luck.

Media notes: Pre-roost audio recording or American crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon.
Observation Details: Count of nocturnal roosting American crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon.  The count was done by estimating by 10s in smaller or less densely populated trees, or by 50s or 100s in larger trees. This count was lower than the previous week's, which was lower by nearly 50% from the weeks prior. The number of blocks with roosting crows was 18, which is within the range commonly seen with numbers nearly double. The number of block faces with roosting crows was 50, which is near the highest number observed for much larger counts. Although the number of crows observed has dropped by nearly 50% in two weeks, the geographic area occupied has not contracted, resulting in much lower densities of roosting crows in the areas observed. The pre-roosting areas were much larger than the final roosting area, which has bee frequently observed.

The bird looks like a Fish Crow which is much more common in the county - a large, all black corvid, but the call was the typical, distinctive, non-nasal American Crow "Caw" call. Photos added. The bird was vocalizing while on the Broward County side of the canal. The audio proves the observation. The canal separates Palm Beach County from Broward County. Everything on the north side of the canal is Palm Beach County, and everything on the south side is Broward County. I was birding across the canal in Palm Beach County at the time of the observation – please see: https://ebird.org/checklist/S142606128 However, since this bird was clearly on the Broward County side of the canal at the time of the observation, I wanted to get a Broward County checklist in with audio, as my Broward checklist from earlier in the day with an American Crow does not include audio.

Observation Details:
FHC, submitted 02.02.25
 
 Came downtown to check to see if gulls were on any ice upstream of the dam and found crows instead. Roosting location for the area crows and likely conservative. Had seven thousand seven hundred and eight (7,780) birds, counted by 10s, just from the photos submitted and not all of the trees that the crows were in were photographed. Three trees contained at least 1,000 crows. Other full trees ranged from 380-780 crows. Three additional non-photographed trees would be another 1,000 crows.They were also roosting on top of nearby buildings. More could be seen further in toward the city but are not included in this count. Probably well upwards of 10,000 crows at this roost location.
 
 Coincidentally, that evening ABC 57 news ran a news story on the crows roosting in downtown South Bend 
 https://abc57.com/news/crows-descend-upon-downtown-south-bend-for-another-winter
 
 The crows were roosting along the river and concentrated along the river from Colfax Ave downstream to just past Lasalle Avenue, a distance of only @ 1,000 feet. None were later seen in Leeper Park, just to the north of the roost location. Although I have heard from a bus driver that they have also roosted near Memorial Hospital.
 
 The walkways underneath the roosting locations looked like a Jackson Pollack painting from all of the accumulated fecal matter.
 
 Crows began leaving just a little before sunrise, a little before 0730 hrs, with most seen flying upstream along the river corridor. Others were flying to the north. Those north of LaSalle Ave seemed to stay the latest before disbursing. Approximately 200 left the nearest tree when I appeared, or that would have been a fourth tree with 1,000 birds in the photos. Just super-concentrated like leaves on that tree.
 
 Raucously loud as they prepared to disburse.
 
 The numbers in the nearest tree to me dropped from an estimated 1,000 pre-photo to 800 at 0722 hrs, when the first photo was taken and then dropped to only @ 250 birds at 0731 hrs. At 0750 hrs only 30 birds remained in this tree.

Media notes: Seven crows mobbing a RThawk. You can hear the hawk's call as it flies about 30m to a different tree. The crows become somewhat quieter. Some handling noise early as I set down the iPhone to initiate the recording with a field recorder and shotgun microphone. This recording begins about 45 seconds earlier than the ZoomF3 recording (third upload), and ends at about the same time. Amplified, but not all the way to -3 as the background noise becomes objectionable. I accidentally uploaded the unedited version earlier (the fourth upload) and have left it for comparison.
Observation Details: At first, two or three crows were calling from the southwest corner of the woods, moving from place to place in the upper canopy of one or two trees, They subsequently moved north, still on the edge of the woods, making a great deal of noise. It became apparent that they were mobbing a red-tailed hawk that eventually called and moved further north, to the northwest corner of the central woods. The crows calmed down somewhat, but picked up the harassment at a lower level, then upped the harrassment, causing the hawk to move once again. First three recordings are uploaded in order. The third includes the intense mobbing. I initiated a recording of the intense mobbing with Merlin on an iPhone before starting a recording with a Zoom F3 and a Rode NTG2. The iPhone recording (final uploaded file) thus overlaps the time period of the ZoomF3 mobbing recording, starting about 45 seconds sooner. (I inadvertently also uploaded the untrimmed and unamplified Merlin / iPhone recording (fourth upload) and thus far have left it for audio quality comparison.)

Observation Details: Very abundant throughout hike and time.  Loud and clear caw calls and short squared off tails.  Smaller than nearby ravens.  Near the Sycamore Creek and Verde River junction, many crows were flying out from a stand of cottonwoods.  Most were counted by 10s as they kept flying south, with a few large groups.  I counted about 275 birds going south and then there were still at least 30 more that hadn't flown south.  As I continued, a flock flew in from the west side of the river, and I wasn't sure if they were birds returning who had flown south.  Throughout the hike I commonly saw them as I went further north, but it's hard to say for sure if they had moved around after dispersing in the morning.  At times large groups of them foraged together in close flocks, with mesquite bosque being a habitat they utilized

Census of nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon. eBird map is not fully reflective of areas covered due to closure and re-opening of the tracking program during the outing. The census of close to 14k is consistent with the numbers in late November for 2019 & 2020. In Nov. 2017 the census was just over 5,200 and just under 9,000 in Nov. 2018. It is unknown whether the increase in the census represents an increase in roosting crows, improvements in the census process, and/or crows sometimes roosting in areas not included in the census. A temporarily expanded census area in early 2020 demonstrated 1,000+ crows routinely roosted in the NW Pearl district, north of the current boundary of SW Burnside. The density of roosting crows as an average number per block with roosting crows was 364/block. This is high, but not inconsistent with other observations.

Donald and I speculated that the Crows were entertaining themselves because at this time of year they are not caring for/defending their young.  Large extended families roost together at night, dispersing during the day to hunt. The picture shows the all black plumage, black bill, and legs and feet.  (Plumage can have a purple sheen in direct sunlight). Females and males look the same making gender identification difficult.

Census of nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon.
 
 Weather was 37ºF, partly cloudy, breezy, and dry for the duration of the census. The census began at 12:43 am and concluded at 5:37 am. I continued observing the crows until 6:39 am. The census recorded a total of 8,730 roosting crows in an area of 144 square blocks with crows roosting on 43 distinct blocks, 66 block faces. The crows were quiet and settled with a couple of exceptions: once two crows were heard calling from two different directions at about the same time, and one group of ≈20 crows flew a short distance between roosting locations. An anomaly from nearly all previous observations was a complete absence of roosting crows on Block 95e (SW Park Ave., east side, between Alder and Washington. This anomaly was observed the prior week (November, 15, 2020). Block 95e, which has two modest size trees on the east side at the northern most portion, has been the most consistent roosting location for the entire period of observations, beginning in 2018. The prevalence of roosting crows here, regardless of other locations is approximately 90%. Because of this anomaly I returned to the area after completing the census in order to look more closely at the area as the sky began to lighten. I discovered what appeared to be a dead crow suspended in the crown of the northern most tree of the two. The crow was approximately 12-13 meters up in the tree, or about ¾ of the way up. It appeared to be hanging with its head pointing down and its wings fully extended. The presence of a dead crow has been widely reported to deter crows from returning to an area for long periods of time, so this discovery explained the absence of roosting crows. However, there was no obvious explanation for why the crow had died, nor how it remained suspended in the tree. I then observed crows becoming active until just past 6:30 am from Pioneer Courthouse Square (SW Broadway and Morrison). As previously observed, the crows began to vocalize within several minutes of the start of astronomical twilight (5:35 am on this date), but do not leave en masse until well after the start of nautical twilight (6:10 am on this date). At the time of my departure at near 6:40 am, I had not seen any large groups of crows leaving, but many had begun to relocate as I found a group in the trees on the north side of Morrison near 1st Ave.: this area had been empty of roosting crows during my observation earlier.
 
 There are crows roosting outside of this season’s observation area. I observed hundreds of crows roosting just north of Burnside near Powell’s Books in the area of NW 10th & Couch. The density there suggests, based on last spring’s observations in the Pearl District, that there are likely a thousand or more crows roosting in the Pearl. It is unknown how much the census in the downtown core may fluctuate based on use of the Pearl. And it is also unknown if crows are roosting in other, as yet undocumented, areas of the city.
 
 On November 24, 2020, I returned to Block 95e during daylight hours and confirmed with binoculars that it was a crow in the tree, inverted, wings splayed out, and that one of its feet was trapped in the “v” formed by a small branch where it joined a larger branch at about a 45º angle. As I was observing the crow the owner (Steven Lien) of the retail store on the ground floor of the building with its entry on Park and Washington, began to speak with me about the crow. I asked how long it had been there. Steven explained that four days earlier (Friday, November 20, 2020) he had seen several crows attacking the now dead crow. Steven said that the crow had been alive at the time it was being attacked, and that the attack had taken place in the location of the tree where the crows body was suspended. I observed that this was unusual behavior and Steven reaffirmed that he had seen several crows attacking the crow while it was alive, and that the attack ended with the crow where it had remained. Steven reported that crows had not roosted in the area since that time. I asked Steven if he knew how long crows had been roosting on the block. He said that he has owned a business located there for many years and recalled that 2015 was when the roosting began. He marked the year based on the fact that he had purchased a power washer to clean dropping off the sidewalk in front of his business that year and that the 5-year warranty had just expired.

Observation Details: Audio recordings are of two separate American Crows. Both were perched together in a tree not more than 50 feet from me.  I recorded for about 5 minutes. For most of the time one crow called and then the second crow with the higher voice called for about a minute or two before they both flew off.   I separated the recording into two files in order to separate the recordings of the two birds.  They seemed to be calling to other more distant crows.  The more distant crows called back but I'm not sure they can be heard in the recording.  One crow had a higher voice than the other.  Recordings were made on an iPhone with the RODE Rec App and edited (Normalized and High-Pass Filter) using Audacity.

Media notes: It's too loud and there is some echo from inside the enclosure.  Because of the proximity to other caged raptors, I am not certain whether there are some sounds on this recording that were not made by either of the the two American Crows.  I hope to go back and do another recording with the parabola on the other side of the enclosure, so as to reduce noises from other birds.
Observation Details: Two American Crows were in an enclosure behind the administration building, apparently unable to live in the wild.  I recorded them, using a parabola.  One of the birds did almost all of the cawing, starting out near the front of the cage, then becoming scared of the parabola and retiring to the back from where the other smaller crow was watching warily.  Then the bigger crow (who could fly)  became bolder and returned to the front of the enclosure only a few feet away from the parabola and continued his cawing.  At about 6 minutes there is some bill clicking and some other non-crow sounds, which may have come from the adjacent enclosure where there was a Great Horned Owl, but it is possible all of the sounds were made by the two crows. I couldn't see -- it was dark in the back of the enclosure.   Early in the recording there are some almost musical notes, which I believe were made by the crows.

I saw two individuals carrying nesting materials and apparently initiating a nest in a nearby pine tree. These two interacted with a common raven who was harassing them, and I was able to record their vocalizations, which are submitted with this record.The crows were about 3/4 size of the raven with rounded tails in flight compared the raven's more diamond-shaped tail. I also heard the vocalizations (typical sharp "caw" sound) for three other individuals in the area.

Locality: Jesuit College Preparatory School of Dallas, Dallas US-TX 32.91806, -96.81969

Observation Details:
Crows are often seen flying to the I-64/I-264 interchange just before sunset.  I have also seen streams of them flying in that direction from 5 miles southwest (Greenbrier, Chesapeake) and 5 miles west (I-464).  Today, I decided to visit what I think is their night roost.  I first checked northwest of the interchange on Glenrock Rd/Chambers Ave - in the area of the pedestrian bridge over I-264.  No crows there; but I did see a few flying south of there.  
 
 I then headed to the area southwest of the interchange. On the way there, I saw a steady stream of hundreds of crows flying west to east.  As I parked (on Hicks Ave), crows landed on two light poles and in more than 20 trees covering several blocks.  A random pelican flew over! ...More crows continued to fly in from the west.  A resident's trash can lid made a loud noise, and the crows took flight in circles and vocalized. There were both Fish Crows and American Crows; but predominantly American Crows.  The crows then returned to tree tops, both leafless and deciduous trees.  And, even more crows continued flying in and taking a place in the crowded tree tops. Then, what sounded like a gunshot, caused them to take flight again.  This time it was evident there were many more crows than before.  The quantity and volume of their vocalizations was exhilarating.  At sunset, the stream of arriving crows ended briefly. I started to leave and saw hundreds more - for as far as I could see flying in (still from the west).  I drove six blocks west and found a cell tower covered with crows.  There were crows in the nearby trees and more crows still flying in from the west.  
 
 Quantification: If there were just 50 crows in 20+ trees, that's an easy 1,000; but there were more than 50 in many trees, and the crows were on the light poles and the cell tower. Photos, videos.

Observation Details: A solitary crow calling from the tallest spruce at the point, overlooking Auke Bay.  Its 3-parted calls reminded me of an American Crow (i.e., not a Northwestern Crow).  This time I was quick enough to record it with Voice Memos on my iPhone 5S (hear below attachment), and the calls to my ear sound similar to those in this recording from Florida (i.e., far away from the Puget Sound hybrid-zone):    http://www.xeno-canto.org/197261    The crow finally swooped down towards a few Northwestern Crows resting on a dock, where it kept itself segregated from them and also appeared slightly larger.    From Gibson & Withrow's 2015 Inventory paper:  Corvus brachyrhynchos hesperis.  American Crow.  Nests southeasternmost mainland se Alaska (at Hyder—see Gibson and Kessel 1992). Six specimens UAM.  Corvus caurinus.  Northwestern Crow.  Perhaps conspecific with C. brachyrhynchos, according to Rea (in Phillips 1986) and Sibley and Monroe (1990). At first, two or three crows were calling from the southwest corner of the woods, moving from place to place in the upper canopy of one or two trees, They subsequently moved north, still on the edge of the woods, making a great deal of noise. It became apparent that they were mobbing a red-tailed hawk that eventually called and moved further north, to the northwest corner of the central woods. The crows calmed down somewhat, but picked up the harassment at a lower level, then upped the harrassment, causing the hawk to move once again. First three recordings are uploaded in order. The third includes the intense mobbing. I initiated a recording of the intense mobbing with Merlin on an iPhone before starting a recording with a Zoom F3 and a Rode NTG2. The iPhone recording (final uploaded file) thus overlaps the time period of the ZoomF3 mobbing recording, starting about 45 seconds sooner. (I inadvertently also uploaded the untrimmed and unamplified Merlin / iPhone recording (fourth upload) and thus far have left it for audio quality comparison.)

Observation Details: Very abundant throughout hike and time.  Loud and clear caw calls and short squared off tails.  Smaller than nearby ravens.  Near the Sycamore Creek and Verde River junction, many crows were flying out from a stand of cottonwoods.  Most were counted by 10s as they kept flying south, with a few large groups.  I counted about 275 birds going south and then there were still at least 30 more that hadn't flown south.  As I continued, a flock flew in from the west side of the river, and I wasn't sure if they were birds returning who had flown south.  Throughout the hike I commonly saw them as I went further north, but it's hard to say for sure if they had moved around after dispersing in the morning.  At times large groups of them foraged together in close flocks, with mesquite bosque being a habitat they utilized

Observation Details: 5 crows (3 that were primarily hanging out on building across street, 1 that was hanging out on building caddy corner, and 1 hanging out on building across the street but down on corner overhang) and all were cawing obnoxiously and seemingly in some sort of high tension mode. One specifically kept flying to just above my building and flying away, while cawing very loudly, and at first didn't know what had them so riled up but then a little later, there was another black bird that came swooping from my building roof that did not appear to be on friendly terms with the crows making all the racket at it, and it then just flew up and dove down behind building across street. I think it either was an enemy crow, or possibly a raven. And then soon after that, the crows still didn't seem to be calming down and still doing their aggressive charges from building across street to the roof of my building, and then another big black bird came swooping out from my building roof and flew past really fast headed in same direction as the one previously did. Did not get good look at these two enemy corvids and my photos are not best quality to ascertain whether also crows or ravens, so will put them down in the crow/raven category. The rest of the crows still seemed in high tension mode for a while, but their cawing became less intense/loud and the one that was really going at it, seemed to stop doing the charge attack after the second big black bird flew over and left the area. See photos and audio.

Depuis plusieurs semaines, nous voyons parfois jusqu’à 50 Corneilles d’Amérique venir s’installer dans le boisé derrière notre maison en fin de journée à partir la rue Rivard. Le 14 août, j’avais remarqué une cinquantaine de corneilles traversées le marais et se diriger vers le boisé de la polyvalente la Ruche. Ce soir, je me suis rendu au cimetière qui borde le boisé du côté ouest. J’ai vu environ 120 corneilles arrivées dans le bois et il y avait déjà une bonne trentaine de corneilles que je pouvais voir en bordure de la forêt et sur le sol. Quelques corneilles semblaient s’amuser à se sauter dessus un peu comme le font les chiens qui aiment se taquiner avant de partir à la course. Il y a surement plus de corneille car je vois seulement un côté du boisé.

eBird Notes
 Census of nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon. Numbers remain robust with a wider distribution across the downtown area and a continuation of roosting by ≈1,500 crows in the northwest part  of the city in the Pearl District. The bulk of the downtown portion of the roost has shifted away from the river to the west, including returning to many blocks on SW 5th, 6th, and Broadway, and adjacent areas. An observation of Block 95e (SW Park between Washington and Alder) showed that crows arrived at 6:48 pm, 43 minutes after sunset. Small groups of crows (>10) came and went between 6:48-7:17 pm when I left the observation spot to begin the larger census. There were 20 crows in the area at 7:17 pm vs. 80 at 10:30 pm.

Media notes: The first 3 seconds are calls coming from a single crow that was associated on the periphery with a large flock of 250+ Northwestern Crows.  The next 12 seconds one can still hear the crow, but now occasionally overlapping with Northwestern Crows. The rest of the recording are all Northwestern Crows, to illustrate the difference in the sonograms produced.  The crow was perched at the top of a tall spruce when calling, and the recording captured only the very last moments of its presence, i.e., it had been calling quite a bit prior to the recorder being turned on.  The crow then flew off by itself from the main flock, in a direction tangential to the main flock's movement.  Another late fall American Crow possibility in Juneau?  Amazingly, this record is exactly(!) the same date as last year's observation/recording:  http://ebird.org/ebird/ak/view/checklist/S25996300

Observation Details: Including 1 *beyond property boundary*. Heard one early in the survey from near the creek, NW of the start point. By or before 0830, I saw presumably the same individual perched just E of the property, calling regularly from a dead alder stub S of the creek. A few times I also heard a second individual calling distant, E. The closer individual remained perched and continued to call regularly until 0845, when my approach was apparently too close for comfort and it flew to the top of an alder along the stream near the eastern edge of reach 4, where it called some more. This crow flew upstream from its perch at 0853, and I did not hear or see it again.

Media notes: Seven crows mobbing a RThawk. You can hear the hawk's call as it flies about 30m to a different tree. The crows become somewhat quieter. Then, for a second time, the Hawk calls and moves off. This recording overlaps the previous recording. Some handling noise early as I set down the iPhone to initiate the recording with a field recorder and shotgun microphone. This recording begins earlier than the ZoomF3 recording, and ends about the same time. Amplified, but not all the way to -3 as the background noise becomes objectionable. This is the unedited and unamplified Merlin recording, accidentally uploaded
Observation Details: At first, two or three crows were calling from the southwest corner of the woods, moving from place to place in the upper canopy of one or two trees, They subsequently moved north, still on the edge of the woods, making a great deal of noise. It became apparent that they were mobbing a red-tailed hawk that eventually called and moved further north, to the northwest corner of the central woods. The crows calmed down somewhat, but picked up the harassment at a lower level, then upped the harrassment, causing the hawk to move once again. First three recordings are uploaded in order. The third includes the intense mobbing. I initiated a recording of the intense mobbing with Merlin on an iPhone before starting a recording with a Zoom F3 and a Rode NTG2. The iPhone recording (final uploaded file) thus overlaps the time period of the ZoomF3 mobbing recording, starting about 45 seconds sooner. (I inadvertently also uploaded the untrimmed and unamplified Merlin / iPhone recording (fourth upload) and thus far have left it for audio quality comparison.)

Media notes: Northwestern Crow
Observation Details: Originally x American Crow and 1 Northwestern Crow; updated to x American Crow with eBird's 2021 taxonomic update.

AMCR is present year-round at Sky Valley Resort.
 
 AMCR bill is proportionately shorter than CORA and it is pointed; whereas CORA bill is bulkier.
 
 AMCR call is higher-pitched than CORA: the sound of AMCR is a "caw" which is different from the low-pitched "cronk" sound of CORA.
 
 While flying, AMCR tail is square-tipped; CORA has a wedge-shaped tail, and it is proportionately longer. Also, flight silhouettes differ: AMCR has broad, rounded wings, whereas CORA has angular, slender wings.

Media notes: 3 crows. 1st one cawed from block away, the next two were crows next to each other. There was a fight preceding these calls with another crow or raven.
Observation Details: 6 crows appearing to be part of same group all cawing wildly, and in apparent distress flying around. They were flying from the tree right below my apartment to tree to ledge across the street, down to the tree below that, and then to lamp post. They were all incessantly cawing, and then more would caw in the distance and some would do fast fly by. Then three of the crows all suddenly went and attacked another crow that I've seen before that's missing tail feather or is discolored, and missing a wing feather, that I call Notch, in another tree, (Notch may be a raven, unclear) but then two of the crows went wing to wing combat with Notch, and wrestled around in the tree branches until Notch then flew away, and they then still kept cawing, and appeared very on edge. 2 then flew over by me and perched in tree below me kind of puffed up and still cawing a little, where I got several pictures. I also got some video clips of some of this (but not the fight). Videos currently in wrong format to upload (MOV file), so may need to convert and upload later. But see photos. Update: I will put 2 down as males, since at least 2 got in fight with Notch, which would be a male crow thing to do. I think puffed up fat looking one in photos is a male. Also have since uploaded sound file.

 Weather was 37ºF, partly cloudy, breezy, and dry for the duration of the census. The census began at 12:43 am and concluded at 5:37 am. I continued observing the crows until 6:39 am. The census recorded a total of 8,730 roosting crows in an area of 144 square blocks with crows roosting on 43 distinct blocks, 66 block faces. The crows were quiet and settled with a couple of exceptions: once two crows were heard calling from two different directions at about the same time, and one group of ≈20 crows flew a short distance between roosting locations. An anomaly from nearly all previous observations was a complete absence of roosting crows on Block 95e (SW Park Ave., east side, between Alder and Washington. This anomaly was observed the prior week (November, 15, 2020). Block 95e, which has two modest size trees on the east side at the northern most portion, has been the most consistent roosting location for the entire period of observations, beginning in 2018. The prevalence of roosting crows here, regardless of other locations is approximately 90%. Because of this anomaly I returned to the area after completing the census in order to look more closely at the area as the sky began to lighten. I discovered what appeared to be a dead crow suspended in the crown of the northern most tree of the two. The crow was approximately 12-13 meters up in the tree, or about ¾ of the way up. It appeared to be hanging with its head pointing down and its wings fully extended. The presence of a dead crow has been widely reported to deter crows from returning to an area for long periods of time, so this discovery explained the absence of roosting crows. However, there was no obvious explanation for why the crow had died, nor how it remained suspended in the tree. I then observed crows becoming active until just past 6:30 am from Pioneer Courthouse Square (SW Broadway and Morrison). As previously observed, the crows began to vocalize within several minutes of the start of astronomical twilight (5:35 am on this date), but do not leave en masse until well after the start of nautical twilight (6:10 am on this date). At the time of my departure at near 6:40 am, I had not seen any large groups of crows leaving, but many had begun to relocate as I found a group in the trees on the north side of Morrison near 1st Ave.: this area had been empty of roosting crows during my observation earlier.
 
 There are crows roosting outside of this season’s observation area. I observed hundreds of crows roosting just north of Burnside near Powell’s Books in the area of NW 10th & Couch. The density there suggests, based on last spring’s observations in the Pearl District, that there are likely a thousand or more crows roosting in the Pearl. It is unknown how much the census in the downtown core may fluctuate based on use of the Pearl. And it is also unknown if crows are roosting in other, as yet undocumented, areas of the city.
 
 On November 24, 2020, I returned to Block 95e during daylight hours and confirmed with binoculars that it was a crow in the tree, inverted, wings splayed out, and that one of its feet was trapped in the “v” formed by a small branch where it joined a larger branch at about a 45º angle. As I was observing the crow the owner (Steven Lien) of the retail store on the ground floor of the building with its entry on Park and Washington, began to speak with me about the crow. I asked how long it had been there. Steven explained that four days earlier (Friday, November 20, 2020) he had seen several crows attacking the now dead crow. Steven said that the crow had been alive at the time it was being attacked, and that the attack had taken place in the location of the tree where the crows body was suspended. I observed that this was unusual behavior and Steven reaffirmed that he had seen several crows attacking the crow while it was alive, and that the attack ended with the crow where it had remained. Steven reported that crows had not roosted in the area since that time. I asked Steven if he knew how long crows had been roosting on the block. He said that he has owned a business located there for many years and recalled that 2015 was when the roosting began. He marked the year based on the fact that he had purchased a power washer to clean dropping off the sidewalk in front of his business that year and that the 5-year warranty had just expired.

Strange display by large flock of ~75. Started with only 20. Flock grew by the time I walked around the lake. The whole time, I could hear loud cawing. The flock would fly in a large circle before perching in trees. A loud, synchronized call would sometimes accompany the flock's taking off in another circle; I was able to record one of these calls. This had been going on in the same location for over an hour, and continued as I left. Altogether a jaw-dropping experience.

Observation Details: As Frank and I were gathering our equipment shortly after 7:00, large flocks of crows began dispersing eastward from a roost to the west. I tried counting the first flock by fives and failed miserably, so I estimated 50. I tried counting the second flock by twos and failed miserably, so I estimated 40 (there were two calling Fish Crows in this second flock, which was extremely surprising as I thought the two species didn't associate). After the flocks of 50 and 40 went by, crows were still calling from the roost - maybe 10, maybe 50 or more, there was no way of knowing. I conferred with Frank, and we agreed on 100. Frank also recorded one making a bizarre squalling vocalization. I wondered if it were being eaten, or if the noise was being made by some injured mammal, but then the squalling resolved into typical American Crow calls before starting again.

To my ear, this does not sound like a juvenile crow, nor like a Fish Crow, nor like a Raven.  Maybe the call actually came from an unseen bird, but I have no idea what.

I heard a few crows making a lot of commotion and getting closer and closer. I then observed them chasing a red fox through the neighbors yard! It gets crazier because then a few moments later a protective new mom white-tailed deer with a fawn hidden nearby chased the fox right through my yard.

Merlin is tagging this as rare but the bar chart for this hotspot shows crows up until the week before. We got recordings on the Merlin sound app that identified them as American crows. There were  also ravens present but we believe that our recordings indicate American crows.
 Edit: We originally reported four crows. After being informed that there had been rare sightings of only two known crows on the island, we believe we probably counted the same bird more than once. We had uploaded photos of one of our sightings. I am now uploading two audio files for additional documentation.

Known location for the species in Palm Beach County.  The bird looks like one of our ubiquitous Fish Crows – a large, all black corvid, but the short, non-nasal “Caw” call distinguishes the species.  I took photos and recorded audio. I saw and heard the bird on the Palm Beach County side of the Canal Levee.  This was the only bird I saw or heard; none were on the Broward side.  It flew in from the north.  I later saw the bird around 1 mile to the east, as I was driving home.
 
 The audio isn’t great, but it should be good enough to prove the observation.

Observation Details: Starting with an estimate of 60 birds there when I arrived, most of these were counted individually or by 2s and 5s (occasionally by 10s when the groups were heavier) as wave after wave flew in,  principally from over the southern end of Ross Island to the construction site on South Waterfront. As I headed back to the car I noticed that more had been coming in from over Marquam Hill behind me. Very easy to imagine that over 2000 crows had converged on the area by the time I left; more were straggling in. It was a marvelous din.

I have heard this crow many times this summer and have finally got around to trying to get audio of it. It is very distinct and this recording is the only vocalization that I have heard that appears to be from this individual.

Media notes: Crows vocalizing at approximately 8:30 pm on SW Morrison between 2nd & 3rd avenues.
Observation Details:
February 17, 2020
 Monday
 7:06 - 11:00 pm
 48ºF, clear, dry, calm
 
 Nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon. This is observation one of two for the night. Emily Midyette is now assisting us and did a simultaneous observation of the NW Pearl district, generally north of Burnside and between NW 3rd and 15th avenues, north to Overton. The NW Pearl census was 1,100 roosting crows. The roost in the downtown core (see map) has largely moved north away from Tom McCall Waterfront Park near the SW Naito and Clay, and is focused north of the Morrision bridge and then west into the city. In addition to roosting in trees, there were ≈100 crows roosting on the parking lot at Naito and Harvey Milk, and ≈250 crows roosting on the roof of the building at Block 261 (SW 2nd and Harvey Mile). This was the first census on a Monday since September, 2019, and I found a substantial number of crows along 4th, 5th, & 6th avenues between Oak and Morrison. It is potentially due to the hawk hazing schedule and Monday’s being between hazing, but this is speculation since the people responsible refuse to disclose the hazing dates. This observation also saw more crows in the central core and fewer than have been found recently in the Pearl. Block 95 (SW Park at Washington) continues to be a persistent roost area regardless of other areas being used. I observed the area from before roosting until the first crows arrived. The beginning of astronomical twilight was at 6:15 pm. At precisely this time I saw a group of ≈100 crows fly overhead but not land. At 6:17 pm a single crow landed in a tree at the block. Two minutes later, seven more crows joined it.

Observation Details: One regular-sounding crow, plus a vocalization heard multiple times that I could not identify at the time of observation. Each time, it was a sequence of the same short, harsh, sound three times, repeated twice.  One suggestion was mockingbird, but it doesn’t seem quite a match to me.  Makes me think of a pheasant or similar.  In some ways similar to cuckoo.  Audio recording. Best examples of the call are in the recording around 35 and 42 seconds (less background noise.). Note: subsequently, I heard what I think is the same sound on 3/11/20 in Essex: https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/214864671.  Subsequently to that, Sean Williams said he had identified this vocalization previously as an American Crow (it is a amazing to me this sound could come from a crow!). Then, on 3/26/20, while visiting island road in essex again, I saw a crow making this same sound, over and over again! Please see the following for more info: https://ebird.org/checklist/S66233052. Thanks to Sean, Marshall, and Jeremiah for helping identify this sound!

Media notes: What sounds like white noise is actually hundreds of crows!
Observation Details:
Crows are often seen flying to the I-64/I-264 interchange just before sunset.  I have also seen streams of them flying in that direction from 5 miles southwest (Greenbrier, Chesapeake) and 5 miles west (I-464).  Today, I decided to visit what I think is their night roost.  I first checked northwest of the interchange on Glenrock Rd/Chambers Ave - in the area of the pedestrian bridge over I-264.  No crows there; but I did see a few flying south of there.  
 
 I then headed to the area southwest of the interchange. On the way there, I saw a steady stream of hundreds of crows flying west to east.  As I parked (on Hicks Ave), crows landed on two light poles and in more than 20 trees covering several blocks.  A random pelican flew over! ...More crows continued to fly in from the west.  A resident's trash can lid made a loud noise, and the crows took flight in circles and vocalized. There were both Fish Crows and American Crows; but predominantly American Crows.  The crows then returned to tree tops, both leafless and deciduous trees.  And, even more crows continued flying in and taking a place in the crowded tree tops. Then, what sounded like a gunshot, caused them to take flight again.  This time it was evident there were many more crows than before.  The quantity and volume of their vocalizations was exhilarating.  At sunset, the stream of arriving crows ended briefly. I started to leave and saw hundreds more - for as far as I could see flying in (still from the west).  I drove six blocks west and found a cell tower covered with crows.  There were crows in the nearby trees and more crows still flying in from the west.  
 
 Quantification: If there were just 50 crows in 20+ trees, that's an easy 1,000; but there were more than 50 in many trees, and the crows were on the light poles and the cell tower. Photos, videos.

Observation Details: Leaving roosts?

The crows were together on the parking lot pavement, in many treetops, on rooftops and on utility lines. Singles, pairs and sometimes many shifted between these locations. Lots of variety of brief vocalizations followed by longer periods of silence. No Fish Crow vocalizations. I sat with them for 30+ minutes. Heard distant American Crow vocalizations east of here. It is 2.25-2.75 hours before 7.30 AM sunrise. I came here this morning because last night: I saw crows flying in this direction 20-30 minutes before sunset. Usually they fly northeast (to the 64/264 interchange night roost). Less frequently they fly here and south of here (on Crossways Blvd). After this (and before 6AM), I checked Crossways Blvd and found NO crows. (Poor photos were with cell phone, except for last two.)

Revised February 6, 2022. A review of digital photos demonstrated that I likely under-estimated at least 10 trees by at least 200 crows (see photo with digital markup). Based on this review I have revised the total census up by 2,000 to a total of 16,500.
 
 February 2, 2022 - Wednesday
 44ºF, cloudy, light rain, winds calm
 Observation time: 6:36 pm - 9:46 pm
 Duration: 3:10
 Distance traveled ≈ 5.5 miles
 Census: 14,500
 
 The vast majority of the roosting crows were present between the Willamette river and SW 2nd Ave. (e/w), and SW Oak to SW Jefferson (n/s). Not all core areas were observed, so some minor numbers may not have been included. I made incidental observations in the NW Pearl District, north of Burnside and observed approximately 600 roosting crows, but they are not included in the observation data for the downtown core. By the time I completed my observations at 9:46 pm at least half of the crows that had been roosting between the river and Naito Pkwy had left, leaving the area from the Morrison bridge south to Salmon largely empty of crows. I did not notice a mass movement of crows so do not know where they went. Unusually, there was extensive roosting on the Hawthrone bridge, including all horizontal parts of the infrastructure, starting with the western most counterweight tower going west to where the supports curve down to the roadway. I watched a group of approximately a dozen crows for about 30 continuous minutes. They were perched on a cross member of the west counterweight tower and about 20 meters above the bridge deck. Except for individual small movements (e.g., stretching a leg, lifting a wing, brief preening) they were still and appeared to be resting. None of the birds in this group vocalized. They were 2-3 “crow widths” apart on the cross member and did not interact with one another. At approximately 9:30 pm the bridge did a brief lift with the center portion rising and then lowering. Most of the crows that had been roosting on the bridge flushed and flew west even though the portion of the bridge being raised had not been part of the roosting area. The flashing lights, moving traffic gates, moving couterweights, etc. appeared to cause the crows to leave. I did not remain to see if they returned. The census of 14,500 was greater than the two most proximate censuses from 2020: January 31, 2020 = 11,690 and February 7, 2020 = 12,990. The average of these two is 12,340, making tonight’s census (excluding the NW incidental observation) approximately 17% greater.

Census of nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon. The documented number (12,500) is well below the 17,000+ found during the November census. Possible explanations include fewer crows roosting in Portland, or secondary or tertiary roosting sites nearby. Historically there have been well over 1,000 roosting north of the primary observation area in the Pearl district. A commenter on a social media (Instagram) post noted a significant number of crows further south on the waterfront during the pre-roosting time. This observation found the crows at the southern end of the observation area and in trees immediately adjacent to the Willamette river. Unusual, but not undocumented roosting locations included the grass near the river and extensive use of utility wires over SW Naito Parkway and adjacent streets and areas. The wires are largely temporary due to road and sidewalk construction. Small flights of crows were observed early in the observation period but the vast majority were settled and quiet, including crows on utility wires no more than 20’ above an active and busy roadway. Weather conditions were 40ºF, breezy, cloudy, and dry.

Observation Details: Usually I hear one or two Crows as they flyover or are nearby.  They may come in the yard but I see them staying here infrequently.  This was indeed a treat although I hope they are okay,  One of the adults seemed to have areas or patches around the head that the feathers were rough or removed.  I did see the parent birds show lots of affection toward the youngest bird.  Perhaps the young bird just needed to rest.  I watched them for 35 minutes.  At first, I heard an adult Crow communicating with a younger Crow sitting in one of the Maple trees here.  It seemed the adult was across the street calling to the younger bird.  Later after recording the young Crow's ahh sounds, I observed from inside and saw 2 young Crows being tended to by an adult and another adult Crow came over to them. So there were 4.  Then I saw one of the young juveniles was facing east on mid branch of the Maple tree and the 2 adults were on either side of that young bird.  Those 3 Crows were facing west.  The bird in the middle by the parents seemed a little more immature than the bird facing east.  Then, one of the adults came down to the bird bath and got water.  The bird bath is now on the ground possibly due to an opossum trying to get water.  It has been dry here. The crow perched on high branch above the bird feeders.  It made a soft call that I would describe as "LADLADLADL".  It repeated it several times.  Each time bowing and fanning the tail.  Once I observed a beak click after the call as well.  
 I have been hearing this very soft call in the forest and trying for a week to identify what it was.  This is the first time I was lucky enough to see the call happening. Merlin does not recognize the sound but I fortunately had Merlin recording at the time so I was able to capture the sound.
 I edited the audio file following the ocenaudio instructions posted here: https://ml-rds-wordpress-prod-s3.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/2023/04/Audio-Editing-in-Ocenaudio.pdf. 
 The sound in question starts at about 2.9 and repeats at 22.4 in this file.
 I notice that once I followed the instruction to do Effects…Amplitude→Normalize, the sound became much softer rather than louder as expected. I still have the unedited original so let me know if I should re-edit and upload that.

Estimate. I heard Crows at a distance when I started my walk. Sounded like they were agitated. They went quiet. Later, the Crows came down the hill in a big mob, calling like crazy. I thought there must be a Great Horned Owl. Crazy Mob! I took a spot and waited, hoping the target would come my way. There was a move and the mob went nuts. There was another move and the mob went bonkers again. I was scanning the tall evergreens but could not find anything. On the third move I caught  glimpse. Pretty sure it was Great Horned Owl. Just a large, dark bird flying low and away from Crows. I lost some time waiting on this. Eventually the whole mass moved down to the tall pines near Beech St.. I tried again to spot but no luck.

Media notes: Crows flushed from trees at about 8:00 pm by a loud traffic noise. Approximately 1,000 crows took flight at once, but settled back into trees within 5 minutes.
Observation Details:
February 17, 2020
 Monday
 7:06 - 11:00 pm
 48ºF, clear, dry, calm
 
 Nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, Oregon. This is observation one of two for the night. Emily Midyette is now assisting us and did a simultaneous observation of the NW Pearl district, generally north of Burnside and between NW 3rd and 15th avenues, north to Overton. The NW Pearl census was 1,100 roosting crows. The roost in the downtown core (see map) has largely moved north away from Tom McCall Waterfront Park near the SW Naito and Clay, and is focused north of the Morrision bridge and then west into the city. In addition to roosting in trees, there were ≈100 crows roosting on the parking lot at Naito and Harvey Milk, and ≈250 crows roosting on the roof of the building at Block 261 (SW 2nd and Harvey Mile). This was the first census on a Monday since September, 2019, and I found a substantial number of crows along 4th, 5th, & 6th avenues between Oak and Morrison. It is potentially due to the hawk hazing schedule and Monday’s being between hazing, but this is speculation since the people responsible refuse to disclose the hazing dates. This observation also saw more crows in the central core and fewer than have been found recently in the Pearl. Block 95 (SW Park at Washington) continues to be a persistent roost area regardless of other areas being used. I observed the area from before roosting until the first crows arrived. The beginning of astronomical twilight was at 6:15 pm. At precisely this time I saw a group of ≈100 crows fly overhead but not land. At 6:17 pm a single crow landed in a tree at the block. Two minutes later, seven more crows joined it.

Observation Details: Shy Guy was identifiable today. Same behavior as the last two years. Grabs one peanut while barely landing and leaves. I think this bird is 3, maybe 4 y.o. this year. One of the younger crows was doing a cool vocalization and I have one semi-clear example. It was a short throaty purr/rattle followed by a quiet, clear, somewhat nasal little honk. The crow did this multiple times, a sibling  showed up, they began “talking” in caws, the sibling left, and the crow resumed the purr/rattle followed by the single quiet honk. I didn’t get audio of the longer series because baby started crying. Update: I had a recording of another rattle from 2023 on the trail by my house (so probably my same little group of birds), but the rattle here is "dryer" sounding and seems to be a bit shorter to me. https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/615409590

Observation Details: Two young ones making begging calls to an adult while all three were on the ground in short grass.  Not sure how recently fledged. Four smaller ones on the ground in the same place a bit later making same calls.  Merlin at first called them Fish Crows, but changed its opinion to American Crow when the calls became more strident.  At least two are very obviously young American Crows and I really think all of them are.  They didn’t fly enough for me to check wing tapering and beats (like I’d know the difference anyway).  Location could support either species.

Media notes: This was a crow/raven sp. with bright white under feathers making clicking noise at a seagull that was hoovering nearby over bay. UPDATE: 3/8/22-Female crow due to clicking call.
Observation Details:
Raven making click calls and then probably it's mate came and they began calling to each other and were making more caw type noises. Audio and photos will be uploaded shortly.
 UPDATE: I just looked at the photos and I don't think these are Common ravens, but I don't know what they are. The one I got the photos of has tons of white inner feathers like Chihuahuan Ravens have, but they supposedly don't live here. And it's beak is not as curvy as a raven's beak, but it's got some long nasal bristles in one photo. And then it has this white furry undercoat that's super fluffy and thick and white, and I cannot find any photos of any American Crows or Common Ravens with similar white feathers underneath that is as contrasting and intense like this bird has. I've also never heard any crows make clicking noises like this bird made but perhaps it's just a first. In any case, since I'm no longer confident in saying this is a common raven, I'm changing this from common raven to crow/raven sp. See photos & audio file.
 UPDATE 3/8/22: (I just learned from reading Dr. Kaeli Swift's Corvid research blog, that the call this hostess cupcake corvid was making is a distinctive call only made by female crows, i.e., the knocking, rattle clicky call in the attached audio file. Based on same, I'm updating this from crow/raven sp. to American Crow, and putting the hostess cupcake crow down as female). Referenced article: https://corvidresearch.blog/2019/03/14/crow-vocalizations-part-ii-qa/#:~:text=Most%20scientists%20describe%20it%20as,female%20crows%20utter%20this%20sound.

Locality: Walker Springs Park & Ten Mile Creek Greenway

Observation Details: Estimate. I heard Crows at a distance when I started my walk. Sounded like they were agitated. They went quiet. Later, the Crows came down the hill in a big mob, calling like crazy. I thought there must be a Great Horned Owl. Crazy Mob! I took a spot and waited, hoping the target would come my way. There was a move and the mob went nuts. There was another move and the mob went bonkers again. I was scanning the tall evergreens but could not find anything. On the third move I caught  glimpse. Pretty sure it was Great Horned Owl. Just a large, dark bird flying low and away from Crows. I lost some time waiting on this. Eventually the whole mass moved down to the tall pines near Beech St.. I tried again to spot but no luck.

Census of nocturnal roost of American Crows (Corvus brachyrhynchos) in downtown Portland, OR. This is the greatest number of this roosting season. The vast majority of the crows are in the south/east portion of the survey area with a small number near the central portion of the area. The crows were largely settled and little movement was seen during the 3+ hours of surveying. I checked the Pearl District, the area north of the downtown core where 1,000+ crows were regularly found roosting in the past, but there were no crows and no signs (i.e., droppings) of recent significant activity. I also checked frequent roosting areas in the north section of the survey area, and at PSU, but no crows were observed. Crows were roosting on the roof of the Keller Auditorium and the top deck of the Crown Plaza parking garage.

One regular-sounding crow, plus a sound I could not identify at time of observation.  Called multiple times.  Each time, it was a sequence of the same short, harsh, sound three times, repeated twice.  Has some similarity to Helmeted Guineafowl. Audio recording. Seems the same call as I recorded on 1/30/20 in Rowley, MA: https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/206295701.  Subsequently, Sean Williams said he had identified this vocalization previously as an American Crow (it is a amazing to me this sound could come from a crow!). Then, on 3/26/20, while visiting this location again, I saw a crow making this same sound, over and over again! Please see the following for more info: https://ebird.org/checklist/S66233052.  Thanks to Sean, Marshall, and Jeremiah for helping identify this sound!

I debated posting this because i know that Fish Crow has never been recorded in NB. I heard (and then saw) a strange sounding crow near the Smythe St. entrance to Odell Park. The call was high pitched, nasally, and more abrupt than a typical AMCR. The audio recording attached is poor quality, taken from my cell phone. I heard the bird again the next morning in the same location. It seems to be hanging around if anyone wanted to come down and try to confirm this report. Im happy to be proven wrong if this is just an odd sounding AMCR. The first recording is from the day of this observation. The second recording is a bit better, taken the next day (15 Apri 2023) in the same location. There is less noise on the second recording and the call is more clear. Update. Thanks to Jim Wilson for identifying this as a female AMCR nesting call.

Media notes: Although you can barely hear it over the waves, you can see it spectrographically: One of the crows was consistently giving a sighing, two-note hoot or low whistle: hooo huuu.
